1	# Rook vs Pawn Endgames
2	
3	> The fight of a lone rook (usually with its king nearby) against a passed pawn — does the rook stop and win the pawn, or does the pawn, escorted by its king, promote?
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
6	**Prerequisites:** [King and Pawn vs King](king-pawn-vs-king.md), [The Opposition](opposition.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	A rook is worth far more than a pawn, yet these endings are razor-thin because a pawn one step from queening is also worth a rook. The result hinges on geometry: how far advanced the pawn is, where each king stands, and whether the stronger king arrives in time. The rook is a long-range stopper — it is happiest **behind** the passed pawn, where it both eyes the queening square and threatens to win the pawn the moment it advances unguarded. The trouble starts when the enemy king shoulders the rook's king away, or when the rook must abandon the pawn to give checks.
11	
12	The single most useful tool is **cutting the king off**. A rook placed on a file or rank the enemy king cannot cross effectively removes that king from the game — letting your own king and pawn do the work, or letting your king march up to round up a lone pawn.
13	
14	## Why it matters
15	
16	These positions appear constantly as the simplification of more complex rook endings. Knowing the exact drawing and winning methods lets you confidently trade down into a won one, or steer away from a lost one. A defender who knows the "rook behind the pawn" technique saves half-points that an untrained player throws away; an attacker who can count tempi stops resigning positions that are actually winning.
17	
18	## Diagrams
19	
20	```
21	8 . . . . . . . .
22	7 . . . . . . . .
23	6 . . . . . . . .
24	5 . . . k . . . .
25	4 . . . P . . . .
26	3 . . . K . . . .
27	2 . . . . . . . .
28	1 . . . . r . . .
29	  a b c d e f g h
30	```
31	*A rook always beats a single pawn that is not close to queening — even with full king support, here Black wins the d4-pawn.*
32	**FEN:** 8/8/8/3k4/3P4/3K4/8/4r3 b - - 0 1
33	Side to move: Black
34	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/8/3k4/3P4/3K4/8/4r3_b_-_-_0_1
35	
36	The rook on e1 ties White to the defense of d4. After 1...Rd1+ the king must step away from its pawn (2.Ke3 Kc4, or 2.Kc3 Rxd4) and Black simply wins the pawn, reaching the trivially won K+R vs K. The pawn here is far from queening, so the rook is in complete control.
37	
38	```
39	8 . . . . . . . .
40	7 . K P . . . . .
41	6 . . . . . . . .
42	5 . . . . . . . .
43	4 . . . . . . . .
44	3 . . . . . . . .
45	2 . . . . . . . .
46	1 . . . . . k . r
47	  a b c d e f g h
48	```
49	*Pawn on the 7th with king support and the enemy king far away: White wins by promoting.*
50	**FEN:** 8/1KP5/8/8/8/8/8/5k1r w - - 0 1
51	Side to move: White
52	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/1KP5/8/8/8/8/8/5k1r_w_-_-_0_1
53	
54	White plays 1.c8=Q. The black rook on h1 is too far to stop it and the black king on f1 is miles away. The rule of thumb: a pawn on the 6th or 7th with its king beside it wins **only if the enemy king cannot reach the queening corner and the rook is not already behind the pawn**. Compare this with Example 2.
55	
56	## Patterns and sub-types
57	
58	- **Rook behind the passed pawn** — the ideal post; the further the pawn advances, the more it must commit while the rook waits to win it.
59	- **Pawn on the 7th, rook on the queening square** — the rook blockades; result depends on whose king is nearer.
60	- **Short side / long side** — in the related Lucena/Philidor family, the defending king belongs on the **short side** so the rook has the **long side** for checking distance.
61	- **Cutting off the king along a file or rank** — turns a small edge into a clean win, or stops the enemy king from escorting its pawn.
62	- **The shouldering / body-check** — the king uses its body to deny the enemy king a key square, a tempo-stealing device.
63	- **Outside checking distance** — three or more files between the checking rook and the enemy king, so the king cannot approach to block the checks.
64	
65	## How to spot it
66	
67	```mermaid
68	flowchart TD
69	  A[Lone rook vs one pawn] --> B{Pawn past the 5th rank}
70	  B -->|No| C[Rook stops it easily and wins the pawn]
71	  B -->|Yes| D{Can my rook get behind the pawn}
72	  D -->|Yes| E[Rook behind pawn holds or wins]
73	  D -->|No| F{Is the enemy king escorting it}
74	  F -->|No| G[Bring my king to round it up]
75	  F -->|Yes| H{Can my king reach the corner in time}
76	  H -->|Yes| I[King and rook stop the pawn]
77	  H -->|No| J[Pawn promotes - too late]
78	```
79	
80	## Worked examples
81	
82	### Example 1 — Cut off the king, then win the pawn
83	
84	```
85	8 . . . . . . . .
86	7 . . . . . . . .
87	6 . . . . . k . .
88	5 . . . . . P . .
89	4 . . . . . . . .
90	3 . . . . . K . .
91	2 . . R . . . . .
92	1 . . . . . . . .
93	  a b c d e f g h
94	```
95	*The f5-pawn is supported only by Black's king. White's rook plus king overwhelm it.*
96	**FEN:** 8/8/5k2/5P2/8/5K2/2R5/8 b - - 0 1
97	Side to move: Black
98	
99	Black cannot hold the pawn. After 1...Kxf5 White still has rook against a bare king — but more instructively, if Black tries to keep the pawn, the rook cuts the king while the white king approaches: 1...Ke5 2.Re2+ Kd5 3.Kf4 and White scoops the pawn next move. The lesson: a rook plus an active king beat a single pawn whenever the rook can sever king from pawn.
100	
101	### Example 2 — The rook behind the pawn draws
102	
103	```
104	8 . K . . . . . .
105	7 P . . . . . . .
106	6 . . . . . . . .
107	5 . . . . . . . .
108	4 . . . . . . . .
109	3 . . . . . . . .
110	2 . . . . . . . .
111	1 r . . . . . k .
112	  a b c d e f g h
113	```
114	*The pawn is one step from queening, but the rook sits behind it on the queening file — only a draw.*
115	**FEN:** 1K6/P7/8/8/8/8/8/r5k1 w - - 0 1
116	Side to move: White
117	
118	If 1.a8=Q Rxa8+ 2.Kxa8 the game is a bare-king draw. White can shuffle the king (1.Kb7 Rb1+ 2.Ka8 Ra1, etc.) but can never both shield the pawn and free it from the rook's gaze. This is the defender's lifeline: **put the rook behind the passed pawn on its queening file** and it is held. Contrast Diagram 2, where the rook was *not* behind the pawn and the pawn ran through.
119	
120	## Common mistakes
121	
122	- Placing the rook **in front** of an advanced pawn (passive blockade) instead of behind it.
123	- Giving checks from too close, letting the enemy king step toward the rook and block.
124	- Letting your own king sit on the **wrong side**, blocking its own path to the pawn or corner.
125	- Forgetting the **shouldering** resource — using your king to deny the enemy king a key square.
126	- Trading into a rook-vs-pawn ending without counting tempi; a single move decides win versus draw.
127	
128	## Train it
129	
130	- Drill the "rook catches the pawn" race: set a pawn on the 6th with no king help vs a lone rook far away and count, both colors, 10 reps, until the tempo count is instant.
131	- Practice **cutting off the king** with rook + king vs lone pawn, 10 reps each side, converting to K+R vs K.
132	- Practice the **rook-behind-the-pawn draw** (as in Example 2) from both sides, 5 reps.
133	- Lichess puzzle themes: `rookEndgame` and `endgame`; use **Practice with computer** for the endgame drills.
134	- Books: Dvoretsky, *Dvoretsky's Endgame Manual* (rook vs pawns); Silman, *Silman's Complete Endgame Course* (rook-endgame sections by rating band).
135	- Verify any tricky position on the **Lichess endgame tablebase** for the exact result.
136	
137	## Exercises
138	
139	1. **FEN:** 8/8/8/8/4k3/4p3/8/4K2R w - - 0 1 — *White to move.* Can White stop and win the e-pawn? How?
140	2. **FEN:** 8/2K5/2P5/8/8/8/7r/3k4 w - - 0 1 — *White to move.* Does the pawn promote, or is it a draw?
141	3. **FEN:** 8/8/8/3k4/2p5/8/2K5/R7 w - - 0 1 — *White to move.* Win the pawn.
142	4. **FEN:** 7k/8/8/8/8/1p6/1K6/r7 w - - 0 1 — *White to move.* White is up a rook — what is the result, and what is the trap?
143	
144	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
145	
146	1. **Yes, White wins.** 1.Kd2! defends nothing but attacks e3 and blocks the pawn's escort. The black king cannot hold the pawn alone: 1...Kf3 2.Rf1+ Kg2 3.Re1 and the pawn falls, leaving the easily won K+R vs K. The active king plus rook dominate the lone pawn.
147	2. **Draw.** The pawn is on the 6th but the black king on d1 and rook on h2 combine to stop it, and White's king cannot escort it home in time. After 1.Kb7 Rb2+ 2.Ka8 (or 2.Kc8 Rc2) the rook keeps checking / pinning and the pawn never queens. With best play it is a draw — a 6th-rank pawn is not automatically winning.
148	3. **1.Kc3!** The rook on a1 holds the long file behind the pawn's potential path while the king attacks c4. Black cannot defend it (the king on d5 is needed to stop the rook side), so 2.Kxc4 follows, reaching K+R vs K. A rook plus king always beat a single non-advanced pawn.
149	4. **Draw — beware the stalemate trap.** Correct is **1.Kxa1!** when after 1...b2+ 2.Kxb2 it is bare king versus bare king. The trap: 1.Kxb3?? or 1.Kc3?? lets Black untangle and the position is no longer a simple draw. Take the rook with the king and accept the K vs K draw; do not grab the pawn first.
150	
151	</details>
152
